verses.text: And the LORD said unto Samuel, How long wilt thou mourn for Saul, seeing I have rejected him from reigning over Israel? fill thine horn with oil, and go, I will send thee to Jesse the Beth-lehemite: for I have provided me a king among his sons. Guidance: 'How long wilt thou mourn for Saul?' God's question is tender, not impatient. Samuel had been weeping for the king he had to dismiss (15:35); God lets the grief stand for a while and then says, gently, that grief cannot become permanent residency. There is work to do. Notice the verb 'I have provided' (ra'ah, literally 'I have seen / chosen for myself') — the chosen successor is already a real person, already known to God, already living in a particular village. The transition from old king to new is not a vacancy God is anxiously looking to fill; it is a quiet handoff God has already arranged in his providence. 'Bethlehem' makes its first significant entry into Israel's royal story; from this small village will come not only David but, in time, the Son of David. The horn of oil that Samuel is told to fill is a deliberate contrast to the small vial used for Saul (10:1) — the difference between a temporary appointment and a lasting dynasty.

And Samuel said, How can I go? if Saul hear it, he will kill me. And the LORD said, Take an heifer with thee, and say, I am come to sacrifice to the LORD. And call Jesse to the sacrifice, and I will shew thee what thou shalt do: and thou shalt anoint unto me him whom I name unto thee. Guidance: Samuel's fear is not faithless; it is realistic. Saul's mental state has degenerated to where a public anointing of a rival would be a death sentence. The LORD does not rebuke Samuel for honest risk-assessment; He provides the cover: take a heifer along, host a sacrifice at Bethlehem, and anoint the chosen son inside that gathering. Note that the cover is not a lie — Samuel really does perform a sacrifice at Bethlehem; it is simply not the whole reason for the trip. There is a category of wisdom that obscures part of a true thing for the safety of the venture, and this is one of the cleanest biblical pictures of it. God does not require His servants to publish every move; He sometimes asks them to do the next right thing under a partial cover and trust Him with the rest of the truth. 'Him whom I name unto thee' — Samuel will not pick the king himself; he will be told. Even great prophets do not choose successors; they receive them.

And Samuel did that which the LORD spake, and came to Bethlehem. And the elders of the town trembled at his coming, and said, Comest thou peaceably? And he said, Peaceably: I am come to sacrifice unto the LORD: sanctify yourselves, and come with me to the sacrifice. And he sanctified Jesse and his sons, and called them to the sacrifice. Guidance: Bethlehem is a small village; a famous prophet's arrival is news the elders cannot afford to misread. 'Trembled' is the right verb — Samuel's visits in the recent past have been judgment visits (the hewing-Agag chapter in particular is fresh). Their first question, 'comest thou peaceably?,' is the village's plea: are we in trouble? Samuel's answer is the deeper truth than they realize: 'Peaceably.' He is in fact bringing the man whose throne will, more than any other, define Israel's idea of shalom. The peace is bigger than they could yet name. Samuel performs the proper purification rites with Jesse and his sons and invites them to the sacrifice. This is the only time in the chapter the surface story and the secret story coincide perfectly — the sacrifice really is happening, the family really is being honored. God often hides the most important business of a day inside what looks like an ordinary religious gathering.

And it came to pass, when they were come, that he looked on Eliab, and said, Surely the LORD's anointed is before him. But the LORD said unto Samuel, Look not on his countenance, or on the height of his stature; because I have refused him: for the LORD seeth not as man seeth; for man looketh on the outward appearance, but the LORD looketh on the heart. Guidance: Eliab is Jesse's firstborn, and Samuel's reaction is immediate and admiring — this man looks the part. The Hebrew suggests Eliab was tall, the same trait that had recommended Saul ('from his shoulders upward higher than any of the people,' 9:2 and 10:23). Samuel, even after the Saul disaster, is still primed to look for the same surface features. God interrupts him with one of the most famous corrections in scripture: 'man looketh on the outward appearance, but the LORD looketh on the heart.' The verse is not anti-aesthetic; it is anti-instinctive. God is not against tall, handsome people; He is against the unexamined human reflex of confusing what one can measure with what one needs to know. Israel has just learned this lesson with Saul; the prophet himself is about to repeat the same mistake. The LORD's correction also implicitly redefines who can be king: not the one the village would have picked, but the one whose heart is right. Heart-shaped leadership is a category of qualification you cannot see from the outside; only God can certify it. Note: Eliab is going to resent David for the rest of his life; you will hear him sneer at his little brother in chapter 17. God knew the heart issue at this moment, and the chapter 17 scene will confirm it.

Then Jesse called Abinadab, and made him pass before Samuel. And he said, Neither hath the LORD chosen this. Then Jesse made Shammah to pass by. And he said, Neither hath the LORD chosen this. Again, Jesse made seven of his sons to pass before Samuel. And Samuel said unto Jesse, The LORD hath not chosen these. Guidance: A slow, dignified parade. Jesse brings each son forward in birth order; Samuel waits for the LORD's word on each one. Abinadab — no. Shammah — no. Through all seven. The narrative slows down deliberately because the not-choosing is itself a teaching. God's selection of David is going to be confirmed by everyone else's being passed over; the brothers' presence and their rejection make David's eventual choosing unmistakable. Imagine the awkwardness in the room — seven adult sons each presented and dismissed in front of family and village. But Samuel does not yield to social pressure to just pick someone. The chapter is teaching the prophet (and us) that the LORD's choosing has its own pace and is not subject to the human urge to wrap up the search efficiently. Sometimes the only obedient prayer in a search is 'wait.'

And Samuel said unto Jesse, Are here all thy children? And he said, There remaineth yet the youngest, and, behold, he keepeth the sheep. And Samuel said unto Jesse, Send and fetch him: for we will not sit down till we be come hither. Guidance: 'Are here all thy children?' Samuel has to ask. Jesse — the father of the chosen king — apparently did not think the youngest worth including when the prophet came calling. The reply is almost casual: 'there remaineth yet the youngest, and, behold, he keepeth the sheep.' David is missing from his own anointing because his father did not consider him a candidate. Two things land: (1) Jesse, like Samuel and the village, has been thinking about leadership in the wrong category. The 'real' sons are the strong, available, in-the-house ones; the shepherd boy is left in the field. (2) God's chosen king is found doing exactly the work that the LORD has been doing for Israel all along — shepherding. Samuel's reply has a quiet command in it: 'we will not sit down till he come hither.' The whole sacrificial meal is paused until David arrives. The prophet's authority refuses to let the ceremony close without the LORD's chosen one present. There is a kind of fierce loyalty God's servants owe to those God has named; we will not sit down without them.

And he sent, and brought him in. Now he was ruddy, and withal of a beautiful countenance, and goodly to look to. And the LORD said, Arise, anoint him: for this is he. Guidance: David is fetched from the flock and brought into the room. The narrator gives us his appearance: 'ruddy' (reddish, suntanned, healthy-looking), with 'a beautiful countenance' and 'goodly to look to.' Interestingly, after the verse 7 lesson about not looking at appearances, David turns out to look the part too — God's heart-choice is not visually unimpressive, but the appearance is now narratively reframed as a happy bonus, not the basis of choosing. 'And the LORD said, Arise, anoint him: for this is he.' The voice of the LORD inside Samuel speaks the simplest possible designation: this one. The chapter has built the suspense through eight wrong sons, a missing youngest, and a meal on pause, only to land on three short words. God's most consequential decisions often arrive in shockingly plain language; the spectacle is always in the consequences, not the moment of choice.

Then Samuel took the horn of oil, and anointed him in the midst of his brethren: and the Spirit of the LORD came upon David from that day forward. So Samuel rose up, and went to Ramah. Guidance: 'In the midst of his brethren' — the anointing is not private; the seven older brothers, who had each just been passed over, watch the horn pour out on the boy they sent to mind sheep. (This will not produce family harmony.) The narrator says two more things briefly. First: 'the Spirit of the LORD came upon David from that day forward.' For Saul the Spirit had come and gone (it came in 10:10, came again in 11:6, and is about to depart in v14); for David it is permanent. The continuity is the gift. Second: 'Samuel rose up, and went to Ramah.' The prophet does not linger to coach the new king, set up a court, or stage-manage David's rise. He has done his job and disappears. God will do the formation work Himself, in the wilderness years, with sheep and harp and a hunted exile's wandering. Sometimes the most faithful thing a kingmaker can do is anoint and leave.

But the Spirit of the LORD departed from Saul, and an evil spirit from the LORD troubled him. Guidance: A single, devastating verse on the other side of the same Spirit. The very Spirit who has just settled on David has now departed from Saul. The cause is not a freakish supernatural ailment; it is the moral consequence of the Amalek refusal in chapter 15. Saul's office was held by God's empowerment; the empowerment has been withdrawn. The 'evil spirit from the LORD' phrase has troubled readers for centuries. The Hebrew can mean a 'distressing' or 'tormenting' spirit; the 'from the LORD' acknowledges that nothing operates outside His sovereignty, but does not make God the author of evil. The simpler reading is that the LORD permits a tormenting force into the vacuum left by the departed Spirit, and that the loss of God's presence in a man's life leaves him exposed to terrors a Spirit-filled person was protected from. Saul's condition is the chapter's other half: David lit up by God's presence, Saul going dark in God's absence. The contrast will define every chapter for the next twenty.

And Saul's servants said unto him, Behold now, an evil spirit from God troubleth thee. Let our lord now command thy servants, which are before thee, to seek out a man, who is a cunning player on an harp: and it shall come to pass, when the evil spirit from God is upon thee, that he shall play with his hand, and thou shalt be well. And Saul said unto his servants, Provide me now a man that can play well, and bring him to me. Guidance: The servants diagnose what they see and propose a treatment: music therapy. 'A cunning player on an harp' will soothe the king when the dark spells come. The remedy is both ancient and medically modern — music's effect on the nervous system has been studied for millennia. It is also theologically interesting that the chosen calming agent is not a sacrifice, not a prophetic visit, not a renewed obedience to chapter 15's command — it is a song. The court is treating symptoms; the cause is left untouched. Saul agrees: 'bring me a man that can play well.' The instinct is right (music helps the soul) but the instinct is also incomplete (Saul still has not addressed the obedience question). A king who cannot bear to hear the diagnosis instead requests a harpist. Sometimes what looks like seeking help is in fact seeking distraction.

Then answered one of the servants, and said, Behold, I have seen a son of Jesse the Bethlehemite, that is cunning in playing, and a mighty valiant man, and a man of war, and prudent in matters, and a comely person, and the LORD is with him. Wherefore Saul sent messengers unto Jesse, and said, Send me David thy son, which is with the sheep. Guidance: One servant — name unknown, role unimportant — knows of David. The recommendation is striking for how complete it is: six attributes in one breath. Cunning in playing (musically gifted). A mighty valiant man (physically strong). A man of war (battle-ready). Prudent in matters (wise in speech and judgment — the same root used of Daniel later). A comely person (good-looking). 'The LORD is with him.' The last detail is the one that explains the first five. The narrator is showing us, by means of an anonymous servant's casual report, that the Spirit's arrival on David in verse 13 has already produced visible fruit. Within days or weeks of his anointing, neighbors are calling David a war-ready, wise, Spirit-filled man — a leap from yesterday's shepherd boy. God's anointing does its work fast when it lands on a heart already prepared. And the very word 'sheep' in the messengers' note — 'send me David, which is with the sheep' — recalls v11. God is about to call the shepherd-king from sheep to throne by way of a harp.

And Jesse took an ass laden with bread, and a bottle of wine, and a kid, and sent them by David his son unto Saul. And David came to Saul, and stood before him: and he loved him greatly; and he became his armourbearer. And Saul sent to Jesse, saying, Let David, I pray thee, stand before me; for he hath found favour in my sight. Guidance: Jesse loads a donkey with bread, wine, and a young goat — the standard gift for a court visit — and sends David off. David stands before Saul; the king 'loved him greatly' and makes him an armor-bearer (one of several Saul keeps; David is the new addition). The verb 'loved' here is the same one used later of Jonathan and David, and of David's relation to all the people of Israel and Judah (18:16). David has, by the LORD's enabling, a wide and uncomplicated love-evoking presence that Saul himself never had. Saul writes back to Jesse asking permission for David to stay on permanently: 'he hath found favour in my sight.' The verse contains the bitter seed of the next ten chapters — Saul loves David now; Saul will hate David later; and the man whom Saul will try to kill is the same man Saul is now requesting to keep close. Sin's progress can turn yesterday's beloved into tomorrow's target without the heart even noticing the angle change.

And it came to pass, when the evil spirit from God was upon Saul, that David took an harp, and played with his hand: so Saul was refreshed, and was well, and the evil spirit departed from him. Guidance: The chapter closes with a small, repeated scene that would have played out hundreds of times over the next months. The king sits in his chamber, gripped by the tormenting spirit; the boy from Bethlehem picks up the harp and plays; the king's body and mind unclench. 'Refreshed' (rawach) is literally 'given breathing room' — the same root as ruach, spirit/breath; Saul's spirit gets space again. The picture is breathtaking in its irony: the LORD's anointed (David, anointed in v13) is calming the LORD's rejected (Saul, rejected in chapter 15) with worship-music, in the very court where the rejected king will eventually try to spear the anointed one. The kingdom transfer is not happening on a battlefield or by coup; it is happening in a music room, very slowly, while the dying king receives unrecognized mercy from the boy who will replace him. God's mercy often runs through the same door He uses for His decisive purpose; both sides of the providence are quietly at work in one small, beautiful, daily act of harp-playing.
